coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence analysis has been regarded as one of the commonly used analytical methods because of its advantages of simple operation, fast response, low cost and high sensitivity. So far, various fluorescent probes, with noble metal nanoclusters, quantum dots, organic dyes and metal organic frameworks as representatives, have been widely reported. However, single fluorescent probe often suffers from some deficiencies, such as low quantum yield, poor chemical stability, low water solubility and toxicity. To overcome these disadvantages, the introduction of cyclodextrins into fluorescent probes has become a fascinating approach. This review (with 218 references) systematically covers the research progress of fluorescent composites based on cyclodextrins in recent years. Preparation strategies, fluorescence properties, response mechanisms and applications in sensing (ions, organic pollutants, bio-related molecules, temperature, pH) and bioimaging of fluorescent composites based on cyclodextrins are summarized in detail. Finally, the current challenges and future perspectives of these composites in relative research fields are discussed.

head.ce:title: Research progress of fluorescent composites based on cyclodextrins: Preparation strategies, fluorescence properties and applications in sensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence analysis has been regarded as one of the commonly used analytical methods because of its advantages of simple operation, fast response, low cost and high sensitivity. So far, various fluorescent probes, with noble metal nanoclusters, quantum dots, organic dyes and metal organic frameworks as representatives, have been widely reported. However, single fluorescent probe often suffers from some deficiencies, such as low quantum yield, poor chemical stability, low water solubility and toxicity. To overcome these disadvantages, the introduction of cyclodextrins into fluorescent probes has become a fascinating approach. This review (with 218 references) systematically covers the research progress of fluorescent composites based on cyclodextrins in recent years. Preparation strategies, fluorescence properties, response mechanisms and applications in sensing (ions, organic pollutants, bio-related molecules, temperature, pH) and bioimaging of fluorescent composites based on cyclodextrins are summarized in detail. Finally, the current challenges and future perspectives of these composites in relative research fields are discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

row.entry: International Agency for Research on Cancer

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis has advantages of simple operation, fast response speed, low cost and high sensitivity, so it is regarded as one of the most commonly used analytical methods [1–3]. To date, fluorescent probes such as metal nanoclusters (MNCs), quantum dots (QDs), organic dyes (ODs), luminescent metal organic frameworks (LMOFs), up-conversion nanoparticles and fluorescence proteins have been widely reported [4–10]. They are applied including but not limited to chemical analysis, bioimaging, diagnosis and disease treatment [11–14]. However, single fluorescent probe often suffers from some deficiencies. For example, some MNCs have disadvantages including low quantum yield (QY), poor chemical stability and low biocompatibility [15]; QDs are limited in some applications due to their toxicity despite high photostability and low photobleaching [16,17]; ODs have the shortcomings such as limited QY and photobleaching [18–20]; most of MOFs are relatively unsafe for in-vivo application due to their inherent toxicity [21]. Overcoming these disadvantages of fluorophores has become the key to their further applications.

Many approaches have been developed to ameliorate deficiencies of the above fluorescent probes by introducing specific additatives, such as polyimide [22], boehmite nanosheets [23] and cyclodextrins (CDs) [24]. Especially, the incorporation of CDs to fluorophores has been extensively studied in recent years. As a typical supramolecule, CD was firstly reported by Villiers in 1891, which is a toroidal form of natural oligosaccharides [25]. Composed of (α-1,4)-linked α-D glucopyranose units, CDs are circular or conical three-dimensional molecules and usually divided into α-, β-, and γ-CD according to the number of glucose units, containing 6, 7, and 8 glucose units respectively [26–28]. Notable features of CDs are that they contain hydrophobic cavities and hydrophilic –OH [29,30], endowing them with the advantages of environmental friendliness, great biocompatibility, low toxicity and easy modification. CDs have shown broad application prospects in many fields such as drug slow release, separation and recognition of enantiomers, synthesis of new materials, catalysis and especially chemical sensing analysis [31–36].

With the support of above advantages of CDs, a number of MNCs, QDs, ODs and MOFs composites based on CDs have been developed by various preparation strategies, exhibiting a series of attractive properties. Compared with single fluorescent probe, the introduction of CDs may improve the water solubility, chemical stability, fluorescence intensity and expand the range in molecular recognition due to their rigid supramolecular structure, hydrophilic –OH and hydrophobic cavities [37,38]. Therefore, researches of fluorescent composites based on CDs (hereafter abbreviated as FC-CDs) have aroused wide concerns in terms of drug delivery and fluorescence sensing, etc [39,40].

In recent years, some reviews have been dedicated to the synthesis and application of CDs-modified nanocomposites, such as CDs-AuNPs and CDs-polymers [41–46], which provide valuable insights into the development and practical applications of CD-based composites. Nevertheless, there are rare reviews focusing on the interesting area of CDs based composite fluorescent sensors. In He et al.’s article [47], construction, mechanism and analytical application of fluorescent probes constructed with five macrocyclic molecules (crown ethers, CDs, calixarenes, cucurbiturils and pillararenes) as the main body were introduced. In Tang et al.’s contribution [48], the applications of inclusion complexes constructed with CDs to the detection of metal ions, anions and molecules were described. However, a detailed systematic overview specifically focused on the recent advances in CDs based fluorescent probes is apparently lacking. Herein, the purpose of the present review is to focus on the specific discussion of FC-CDs and summarize the published studies about their applications in sensing and bioimaging. Firstly, we summarize preparation strategies of FC-CDs including covalent connection, host-guest connection, electrostatic interaction, π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding and metal coordination. Secondly, we discuss their fluorescence properties and fluorescence response mechanisms, followed by introducing their detailed applications in sensing and bioimaging. Finally, possible improvements and challenges of FC-CDs are prospected. We expect that this review will hold the potential to fill gaps and inspire more advanced studies in FC-CDs.

The preparation strategies of FC-CDs can be divided into covalent connection, host-guest connection, electrostatic interaction, π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding and metal coordination [47]. Among them, covalent and host-guest connections are more commonly used; while reports about electrostatic interaction, π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding and metal coordination are relatively few. A summary about the advantages and disadvantages of the above-mentioned connections for fabricating FC-CDs is displayed in Table 1 .

Covalent connection refers to the combination of a single fluorescent probe and CD by forming new covalent bonds in the preparation of FC-CDs. Because of its advantage of strong binding force, covalent connection has been widely used as a preparation strategy of FC-CDs toward sensing and imaging applications [49]. Generally speaking, CDs modified by sulfhydryl (-SH), amino (-NH2), aldehyde (-CHO) and azide (-N3) groups are easy to form covalent bonds with fluorescent probes [50–53]. For instance, M − S chemical bond has a strong binding constant, which is beneficial for the combination of MNCs and –SH modified CDs. Amidation reaction can happen between –NH2 modified CDs and carboxyl (-COOH) group of fluorescent probes (e.g., QDs); Schiff base reaction may occur between –NH2 modified CDs and fluorescent aldehydes/ketones compounds such as ODs. Copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne click reaction commonly exists between –N3 modified CDs and ODs.

Covalent connections include direct and indirect covalent connections. The former is the most common way to form new covalent bonds between fluorescent probes and unmodified or modified CDs. In Xu's study [50], β-CD was employed as a reducing agent to synthesize AuNCs via a one-pot method, by which AuNCs-CDs composites were synthesized. Compared with previous preparation methods of MNCs-CDs [54,55], this strategy avoids multiple surface modifications of AuNCs and prolonged steps, providing a facile way to construct MNCs-CDs. Another AuNCs-CDs probe was prepared with HAuCl4 and –SH functionalized β-CD (SH-β-CD) toward cholesterol sensing [56], in which the formation of Au–S chemical bond endows the prepared FC-CDs with high stability (Fig. 1 A). Moreover, SH-β-CD can also reduce AgNO3 and Cu(NO3)2, benefiting the formation of AgNCs-CDs [57] and CuNCs-CDs composites [1].

In the synthesis process of QDs-CDs, two covalent approaches are commonly used for linking QDs and CDs. The first one is forming covalent bonds between metal ions of QDs and O atom of CDs. As an example, β-CD capped ZnO QDs composites [58] were prepared via the strong covalent interaction between Zn2+ and –OH of β-CD [59]. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra show that the functional groups of β-CD were not destroyed and β-CD was successfully capped on the surface of ZnO QDs. In addition, amidation reaction offers an effective way for connecting QDs and CDs. Activated by N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), QDs with –COOH can react with β-CD [60,61]. For example, –COOH on the surface of N,S-codoped QDs reacts with –NH2 of mono-(6-amino-6-deoxy)-β-CD, making QDs covalently link to β-CD through a –CO–NH- chemical bond [60]. The fluorescence sensor exhibits a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.51 μM when used for identifying testosterone (Fig. 1B). As another attempt, –COOH modified QDs was covalently attached to 6-aminoethylamino-β-CD via EDC/NHS coupling reaction to construct the composites [61], which were used for detecting cholesterol in practical serum samples.

ODs can directly form covalent bonds with CDs containing various functional groups, like aldehyde (-CHO), –NH2 and –N3 groups [52,53,62]. Recently, composites of CHO-β-CD and thiosemicarbazide were synthesized via Schiff base reaction and applied for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ [52]. Likewise, NH2-β-CD and –COOH functionalized tetraphenyl ethylene (TPE) can undergo dehydrated reaction to form composites with amide covalent bond, which can be designed for the determination of trinitrophenol [62]. In another study, N3-β-CD and TPE composites were synthesized via a Cu(I) catalyzed click chemistry method for Cd2+ sensing (Fig. 1C) [53]. To deduce, direct covalent connection proves itself to be a competitive alternative for the synthesis of FC-CDs and further applications in fluorescence sensing.

Indirect covalent connection in preparation of FC-CDs is defined as a connecting method that uses two ends of a linker to covalently connect fluorescent probes and CDs simultaneously. Compared with direct covalent connection, fewer studies have been reported focusing on indirect covalent connection in the preparation of FC-CDs, which may be attributed to the tedious synthesis process of FC-CDs [63]. An attempt was made toward indirect covalent connection of QDs and CDs [64], which was achieved by two –NCO groups of isophorone diisocyanate. The developed fluorescent composites were successfully employed for the detection of Cr6+ in soil.

To sum up, with the advantages of strong binding and structural stability, covalent connection has been extensively reported and aroused more and more concerns in the preparation strategies of FC-CDs. It should be noted that except –SH, –NH2, –CHO and –N3 groups, whether CDs can be modified with other functional groups or compounds for the preparation of FC-CDs is still worth exploring.

Host-guest connection always occurs in systems containing supramolecular hosts like CDs, crown ethers, calix[n]arenes, cucurbit[n]urils and pillar[n]arenes, in which guest molecules and hydrophobic cavities of hosts selectively bind with each other through non-covalent interaction due to the complementary structure and matching energy [65,66]. So far, FC-CDs prepared by host-guest connection have attracted extensive attention. Among MNCs, QDs and ODs fluorescent probes, reports about FC-CDs based on host-guest connection are mainly focused on the composites formed by CDs and ODs rather than MNCs/QDs, which can be attributed to the unsuitable dimensional compatibility of MNCs/QDs to the cavity of CDs. It is embodied in that MNCs consist of a few to several hundred metal atoms with core sizes always below 2 nm [67]; while QDs are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with sizes ranged from 1 to 20 nm [68]. However, the maximum internal size in CDs family is 0.95 nm (γ-CD) [30], which is much smaller than the sizes of MNCs and QDs.

In contrast, various ODs fluorescent probes with appropriate molecular sizes can enter into CDs cavity via host-guest connection [69–71]. For example, as fluorescent guest molecules, coumarin derivatives can form 1:1 composites with β-CD and be employed for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ through intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process [69]. With the merits of water solubility, membrane permeability and nontoxic nature, the above coumarin derivatives-β-CD system can also be potentially applied in living cells. As another example, fluorescent 2:2 complexes formed with stilbeneboronic acid and γ-CD were developed [70], exhibiting selective and sensitive response toward glucose, through which satisfactory application in artificial urine samples was achieved. In addition, curcumin-β-CD (Fig. 2 A), isorhamnetin-β-CD (Figs. 2B), 3 and 5,6-trichlorosalicylic acid-β-CD composites via host-guest connection were used for the detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and urea, respectively, further indicating that ODs-CDs composites possess a wide range of applications in fluorescence sensing and bioimaging [72–74].

As mentioned above, host-guest connection has been widely used in the synthesis of ODs-CDs rather than MNCs/QDs-CDs. To successfully prepare FC-CDs based on MNCs/QDs by host-guest connection, the dimensional compatibility between MNCs/QDs and CDs should be perfectly matched, which may be achieved by controlling the synthesis conditions. Once MNCs and QDs with smaller sizes are successfully obtained, they are expected to enter into the hydrophobic cavity of CDs and form host-guest composites, which can thus promote the related researches of MNCs/QDs-CDs.

Defined as an interaction between oppositely charged ions, electrostatic interaction has also been applied to the construction of FC-CDs despite the fact that its strength is typically only a few percent or a thousandth of covalent bonds strength [75]. Accordingly, the obtained FC-CDs prepared by electrostatic interaction are not as stable as those synthesized by covalent connection. In addition, to achieve electrostatic interaction between fluorescent probes and CDs, it is necessary to modify CDs with charged motifs, which greatly limits its scope of application. So far, only a few works about charged CDs for constructing FC-CDs have been reported.

Chitosan and CD can form positively charged polymer through formaldehyde crosslinking (CTSCD), based on which N-doped QDs-CDs composites were synthesized via electrostatic interaction and employed for the sensing of Mn2+ [76]. CTSCD as a shell not only promises the stability and biocompatibility of N-doped QDs, but also prevents N-doped QDs from being aggregated and oxidized. In another study, the composites consisting of CTSCD and S QDs via electrostatic interaction were also designed and applied to the sensing of Ag+ [77].

Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), a positively charged surfactant, can also be used to modify CDs and then combine with negatively charged fluorescent probes to synthesize FC-CDs by electrostatic interaction. Recently, composites of 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA)-capped CdTe QDs and β-CD coated with CTAB were constructed by electrostatic interaction between cationic heads of CTAB and anionic MPA groups [78]. The composites were employed for the sensitive detection of paraoxon by fluorescence changes. Except above reports, studies about the synthesis of FC-CDs by electrostatic interaction have been rarely reported.

π-π stacking is a weak non-covalent attraction between delocalized π electrons. It is a particular spatial arrangement of aromatic compounds and is generally considered to be the weakest connection between aromatic rings [79]. Due to the weak non-covalent interaction of π-π stacking, FC-CDs assembled in this way can dissociate easily. Thus, the synthesis of FC-CDs via π-π stacking has been rarely reported. Liu and coworkers [80] used covalent connection and π-π stacking to prepare superbenzene-bridged bis(permethyl-β-CD) composites, which were inclined to self-assemble to form columnar mesophases and allowed charge carriers to migrate among π-π stacking (Fig. 3 ). The composites were inclined to aggregate in different degrees with the change of solvent polarity. The composites can discriminatively sense 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), 2,4-dinitrophenol and 1-methyl-2,4-dinitrobenzene, which enables the potential application for the detection of nitroaromatic explosives.

Hydrogen bonding, firstly proposed by Moore and Winmill in 1912, is a dipole-dipole attraction between hydrogen atom and highly electronegative atoms, such as nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine atoms [81]. At present, there are only a few reports about FC-CDs prepared via hydrogen bonding strategy probably because the strength of hydrogen bonds is susceptible to pH, temperature and solvent polarity, etc. [82–84], which leads to the instability of FC-CDs. As a proof-of-concept, N-doped graphene QDs (GQDs) and β-CD composites were prepared [85], and the connection between N-doped GQDs and β-CD is the hydrogen bonding between free –NH2 of N-doped GQDs and oxygen of β-CD. The composites successfully provide a way for the determination of dopamine by colorimetric and fluorometric methods.

Metal coordination interactions are an important type of noncovalent interactions provided by forming metal-ligand coordination bonds [86]. The strength of the coordination bond varies over a wide range and can be comparable or even higher than the strength of the covalent bond, but the bond remains more dynamic and reversible at the same time [87]. Reports about metal coordination based on FC-CDs are in the academic development stage, and mainly focus on the composites formed by CDs and MOFs rather than MNCs/QDs/ODs.

In 2022, Eu-α-CD MOF [88] was produced by applying bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the inducer. The –OH groups of the organic ligand α-CD act as coordinating groups for Eu-α-CD MOF. The O atoms coordinate with Eu3+ as H atoms dissociate from –OH. Besides, Rh6G@NU-1000 and carboxymethyl-β-CD (β-CMCD) composites [89] were constructed for the specific detection of cholesterol in blood. β-CD was modified with carboxymethyl before reacting with NU-1000. FT-IR spectra show that the modified β-CMCD was tightly fixed in NU-1000 due to the stabilization of the coordination bonds between Zr–O clusters and –COOH groups on β-CMCD. Except the above two reports, there are few studies focusing on the preparation of LMOFs-CDs, and more attention should be paid in the future.

Generally, the introduction of CDs into fluorescent probes such as MNCs [50], QDs [90], ODs [91] and MOFs [88] can bring improved water solubility and chemical stability, while the fluorescence intensity tends to be unchanged or enhanced rather than quenched. AgNCs-β-CD [92], MoS2 QDs-β-CD [93] and TPE-β-CD [62] have been reported to own the same level of fluorescence intensity compared with single fluorescent probes. For instance, in Zhu's study [93], when β-CD was embedded with MoS2 QDs, the fluorescence intensity of the latter almost keeps unchanged. β-CD exhibits host-guest interaction with p-nitrophenol (p-NP), a hydrolysis product of methyl-parathion (MP) under alkaline condition. The fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 QDs can be quenched by p-NP through photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process, and this was further designed to construct a sensitive fluorescence probe for MP sensing. However, it should be noted that reports focusing on the comparison of fluorescence intensity between MOFs-CDs composites and single MOFs are still on the way.

Comparatively, fluorescence enhancement after the introduction of CDs is more fascinating. The enhancement can be attributed to ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT), inhibition of aggregation, aggregation-induced emission (AIE), antenna effect, corresponding to MNCs, QDs, ODs and MOFs based FC-CDs, respectively. For MNCs-CDs, LMCT is a common reason of fluorescence enhancement. It happens from CDs to MNCs via chemical bonds because the delocalized electrons of –OH groups abundant on CDs can be donated to the metal core, ultimately resulting in a significant fluorescence enhancement. For example, the maximum emission intensity of β-CD-anchored AuNCs protected by glutathione (GSH) is stronger than that of GSH-AuNCs [94], which can attribute to the presence of electron-rich atoms like O atoms and then promote the process of LMCT through Au–S bonds. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs was significantly enhanced by modifying the surface of GSH-AuNCs with β-CD [95], which improved the sensitivity of the paraquat sensor by more than 4 times (Fig. 4 A).

The luminescence of QDs is often weakened or quenched at high concentrations, a phenomenon known as “concentration quenching”. One of the main causes of the quenching process is the “formation of aggregates”, which is frequently referred to as “aggregation-caused quenching” (ACQ) [96]. The introduction of CDs can inhibit ACQ and recover fluorescence intensity of QDs-CDs to some extent. A reasonable explanation is that β-CD may regulate the size and dispersion of QDs, which is helpful to reduce ACQ and significantly improved the luminescence properties [40]. For instance, β-CD was assembled onto Mn-doped QDs by one step heating method in acidic aqueous solution [97], which made QDs-β-CD more dispersed than QDs alone, and therefore has better fluorescence properties. In addition, the as-prepared QDs-β-CD was successfully used to analyze Co2+ in spiked irrigation water near local factories.

Fluorescence enhancement of ODs after CDs introduction is commonly induced by AIE, typically for TPE-CD composites. Due to the radioactive decay induced by intramolecular motion, TPE in dilute solution usually possesses weak fluorescence [98,99]. Restricting the rotation of TPE to certain units is an effective strategy to enhance its fluorescence. With abundant –OH groups, CDs tend to react with TPE through click reaction and can be regarded as confinement units toward TPE, which contributes to the occurrence of AIE process. As an example, TPE-CD composites were designed by confining TPE on the surface of β-CD via a Cu(I) catalytic 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition click reaction [100]. Compared with TPE, the luminescence intensity of TPE-β-CD in dilute solution is significantly improved, which enables the composites to recognize hydrogenase sensitively and has the potential for applications in biochemistry and medicinal chemistry (Fig. 4B).

Luminescence properties of MOFs are generally proposed from the following aspects: (1) organic ligand luminescence; (2) metal center luminescence, mainly refers to “antenna effect”; (3) charge transfer luminescence including ligand-to-metal charge transfer, metal-to-ligand charge transfer, metal-to-metal charge transfer and ligand-to-ligand charge transfer; (4) guest-induced luminescence, in which substances with luminescent properties are introduced into MOFs such as QDs and fluorescent dyes. These sources enable MOFs to exhibit luminescence properties and show great potential in fluorescence sensing applications [101]. It is worthy to note that although LMOFs-CDs composites have been rarely reported, there is an evidence confirming that CDs can easily generate the antenna effect with lanthanide ions such as Eu3+ via energy transfer, which can significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ [102]. As an example, Eu-α-CD MOF with characteristic fluorescence was constructed through the connection between –OH of α-CD and Eu, and was employed to specifically recognize dopamine in human urine samples [88]. In another report [89], Rh6G@NU-1000-CMCD was constructed for the selective detection of cholesterol in blood serum. The luminescence mechanisms of the sensor can be attributed to the combination of organic ligand luminescence and guest-induced luminescence, i.e., the fluorescence emission from 1,3,6,8-tetra(4-carboxylphenyl)pyrene and Rh6G with luminescent properties, respectively.

Common response mechanisms of FC-CDs toward various analytes include PET [60], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [103], inner filter effect (IFE) [104], AIE [56], ACQ [76] and ICT [105]. Among these mechanisms, PET is the most common mechanism in terms of FC-CDs fluorescence sensing. PET refers to a physical process of inhibiting the electron transfer from the electron donor group or reaction with a target analyte to emit a “turn-on” fluorescence signal [106]. As a proof-of-concept, fluorescence “turn-on” composites based on S,N codoped QDs and β-CD were designed and used for testosterone sensing via PET process [60]. Ferrocenylmethyl trimethylammonium iodide (Fc+) can effectively quench the fluorescence of S,N codoped QDs-β-CD, causing the occurrence of PET. The QDs-β-CD composites were successfully employed for sensitive detection of testosterone (LOD of 0.51 M). In addition, the detection of p-NP, cholesterol, dopamine and cholic acid was also achieved by FC-CDs via PET process [50,61,107,108].

Mechanisms of FRET, IFE, AIE and ACQ have also been used in terms of FC-CDs sensing. FRET is a mechanism describing energy transfer from a donor to an acceptor in a short range (1–10 nm) [109]. The energy transfer efficiency of FRET mainly depends on the suitable distance, the degree of overlap in the spectra and the orientation of the dipole moments of donor and acceptor [110]. For instance, as a FRET probe, ZnS QDs-β-CD [111] was developed for highly sensitive determination of glucose. 3-Hydroxyflavone can enter into the hydrophobic cavity of ZnS QDs-β-CD to form a FRET system, in which the former acts as an energy acceptor while the latter is an energy donor. The sensing mechanism can be attributed to the partial overlap between the absorption spectrum of 3-hydroxyflavone and the emission spectrum of ZnS QDs. In another study, Rh6G@β-CD-LMOF (NU-1000) composites were constructed for cholesterol recognition in blood serum [89]. Free cholesterol competes with Rh6G to form a stable inclusion complex with β-CD and induces the fluorescence restoration of the ligand in LMOF proportionally, which was quenched by Rh6G in advance due to FRET process. Besides, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [112] and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [103] were also used to construct FRET system with FC-CDs for sensing various analytes, like cholesterol and malathion.

IFE mainly occurs in the case that the absorption spectra of acceptors overlap with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores [113]. For example, hydroxypropyl-β-CD capped S QDs can be utilized as an effective fluorescent probe for tartrazine detection via IFE [114]. There is a good overlap between the absorption band of tartrazine and the excitation and emission bands of hydroxypropyl-β-CD capped S QDs, and the fluorescence of S QDs can be seriously quenched by tartrazine via IFE. Furthermore, FC-CDs based on IFE mechanism have also been used to identify acridine orange, chlortetracycline, aflatoxin B1, etc. [40,57,92,104].

AIE is a phenomenon that a molecule or molecular fragment exhibits fluorescence enhancement in the aggregated state due to the limit of intramolecular movement. As for FC-CDs, AIE often occurs in MNCs-CDs and ODs-CDs [115]. Supramolecular fluorometric sensing system of TPE and γ-CD composites was established and applied in the sensing of α-amylase [116]. With the existence of α-amylase, the fluorescence of the composites is enhanced, which is because α-amylase can hydrolyze γ-CD and cause the fluorescence enhancement due to the appearance of one-dimensional aggregates. In addition to TPE-CD composites, AuNCs-β-CD composites were also prepared and applied in cholesterol sensing due to AIE [56].

ACQ means that the luminescence of fluorophore is weakened or even completely disappears in concentrated solution or at aggregation state [117]. The process of ACQ often occurs in QD-CDs because they tend to aggregate easily and induce fluorescence quenching. An example about ACQ mechanism is the fluorescence quenching of β-CD capped ZnO QDs composites caused by Co2+ [58]. When a certain concentration of Co2+ is added into the aqueous solution containing β-CD capped ZnO QDs composites, they will aggregate to form large clusters, and the strong yellow-green fluorescence emission is quenched.

Unlike the above commonly studied five mechanisms in terms of FC-CDs sensing, there are only a few reports about ICT mechanism. ICT refers to the process of internal electron transfer of molecules in the excited state, resulting in the separation of positive and negative charges within the molecule [118]. As an example, the fluorescence of coumarin derivatives included β-CD was quenched [69] due to ICT between the linker and thiazole alkyl groups. The introduction of Cu2+ can prevent ICT and quench the fluorescence of coumarin derivatives composites based on β-CD. In another work [105], 3,3-dihydroxybenzidine and α-CD composites were produced and applied for Hg2+ and Fe3+ sensing followed ICT mechanism.

Fluorescence sensing based on FC-CDs composites may also be the result of a combination of the above-mentioned mechanisms. For instance, acrylonitrile-substituted TPE derivatives and β-CD composites [62] were prepared for the determination of TNP. According to the density functional theory (DFT) calculation, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and fluorescence emission spectra, the reaction mechanism was attributed to the combination of PET and FRET, with the PET mechanism dominating. It is worthy to note that when FC-CDs composites are used for sensing analytes, the fluorescence responses may be not limited to the above mechanisms, and new judgment must be made on a case-by-case basis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of FC-CDs in sensing and bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: FC-CDs present attractive fluorescence intensity and molecular recognition ability, and have been extensively applied for fluorescence sensing and bioimaging. Compared with bioimaging, FC-CDs are more commonly employed for fluorescence sensing. Over the past few years, a variety of FC-CDs have been prepared and reported for sensing inorganic ions, organic pollutants, bio-related molecules, pH and temperature. A summary of FC-CDs in sensing applications is displayed in Table 2 .

Many metal ions are harmful to ecosystem and human health, so it is necessary to sensitively detect the concentration of these ions to monitor the safety of the environment and organism. During the past decades, FC-CDs composites have found their applications for sensing types of metal ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+ Ag+, Au3+, Co2+, Cr6+, Mn2+, etc.

Hg2+, as one of the most toxic ions, can not only pose a serious threat to environmental systems but also damage human metabolism, so it is important to develop efficient strategies for the sensitive detection of Hg2+ [119]. So far, there are many detection methods for Hg2+, among which using fluorometric method to detect Hg2+ by FC-CDs is an important subject [120]. A fluorescence “turn-off” FC-CDs probe was synthesized by combining curcumin and β-CD and used for sensing Hg2+ in drinking water and tap water samples [72]. Due to the chelation between Hg2+ and curcumin-CD, fluorescence quenching of curcumin-CD happens, and the color changes from yellow to colorlessness. Good linearity in the Hg2+ concentration range of 0–45 μM is achieved with the LOD of 5.02 μM. This method provides a cheap, selective and eco-friendly approach for toxic Hg2+ sensing in environmental samples with satisfactory results. Similarly, a ratiometric FC-CDs sensor based on β-CD and CdSe/ZnS QDs was designed through host-guest interaction [121], by which the detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ was achieved with low LOD of 1.6 and 2.74 μM, respectively (Fig. 5 ). Besides, a white light emitting sensor was constructed by adjusting the ratio of β-CD-CDs to CdSe/ZnS QDs, which is favorable for clearly observing fluorescence changes and can thus bring a prospect for the application in the field of optoelectronics.

Copper group elements include Cu, Ag and Au, and their cations are not as serious as Hg2+ in polluting soil environment, but the consequence of their accumulation is not negligible [122]. Up to now, ODs-CDs are the main FC-CDs composites for detecting Cu2+. The fluorescence sensing is commonly based on the chelation between Cu2+ and S, N or O atoms in ODs-CDs, which leads to the signal change of ODs-CDs [73]. Recently, composites of β-CD and thiosemicarbazide possessing strong blue fluorescence were designed for sensing Cu2+ [52]. S and N atoms in thiosemicarbazide can both involve in the combination of thiosemicarbazide and Cu2+, which induces the fluorescence quenching. Moreover, 4-((benzo[d]thiazol-2-ylthio) methyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-2H-chromen-2-one included β-CD composites [69] was synthesized, which can be used to quantitatively and sensitively determine Cu2+ with low LOD of 0.252 nM. Interference experiments reveal that the composites possess ideal selectivity for Cu2+ among various cations. Due to the nature of the composites including high water solubility, selectivity and low toxicity, this approach successfully provides a potential way in monitoring the biological functions of Cu2+.

Ag+ is one of the most common dangerous environmental pollutants and easily causes many pathologic diseases, like cytotoxicity and organ failure. MNCs-CDs and QDs-CDs exhibit great potential for Ag+ sensing. For example, composites based on CTSCD and S QDs were prepared by self-assembly for high sensitivity detection of Ag+ [77]. The bridge S–Ag–S bonds between S QDs and Ag+ contribute to the aggregation of the composites, leading to fluorescence quenching by ACQ. The composites have the advantages of low cost, hypotoxicity, good water solubility and low LOD toward Ag+, which may have a broad application prospect in the fields of sensing and bioimaging. In addition, most coexisting metal ions do not interfere with the sensing of Ag + except Ni2+ ions, which have a weak effect on detection. In Chen's study [123], AuNCs-β-CD composites can selectively and sensitively detect the concentration of Ag+ in Min river sample. This strategy exhibits a wide linear range of 0.5–100 nM and low LOD of 0.3 nM for Ag+ detection. Moreover, the authors employed ICP-MS method to confirm that AuNCs-β-CD composites are reliable for Ag+ sensing in environmental samples.

Although tiny amounts of Au3+ seem to be less harmful to environment and organisms, it can accumulate in human body and cause negative long-term effects [124]. So far, few researches about Au3+ sensing by FC-CDs have been reported. In 2022, mono-(6-diethylenetriamine-6-deoxy)-β-CD composites were prepared to detect Au3+ and I− consecutively [124]. With the existence of 15 kinds of cations and 12 kinds of anions, only Au3+ and I− can significantly decrease the fluorescence intensity of the composites, implying the good selectivity and anti-interference capability for the sensing of Au3+ and I−. The calculated LOD of mono-(6-diethylenetriamine-6-deoxy)-β-CD composites toward Au3+ is 2.73 μM. This work provides an attractive approach for the application of FC-CDs as a fluorescent “on-off” sensor in highly selective and continuous ions detection.

Co2+ is an essential oxidation-reducing ion involving in many biology processes in living organisms, but causes some health problems when it is found in large amounts. It is often found in trace amounts in environmental and food samples, therefore, it is necessary to develop accurate and sensitive analytical methods to detect trace amounts of Co2+ [125]. For instance, composites of β-CD and GSH-AuNCs were designed and applied in sensing Co2+ and imaging in L929 cells [126]. S atoms easily combine with Co2+ to form complex, leading to the interrupt of the charge transfer process through the Au–S bond and causing the decreased fluorescence intensity. Additionally, the composites possess red-luminescence and great biocompatibility, showing deep penetrating ability and great potential for Co2+ sensing in biomedical devices. Besides, QDs-CDs such as ZnO QDs-β-CD and Mn-doped ZnS QDs-β-CD can also be used for the detection of Co2+. For example, ZnO QDs-β-CD was designed [58] and employed to detect Co2+ in tap water and lake water, showing the merits including good selectivity, simplicity and nontoxicity. Mn-doped ZnS QDs-β-CD [97] was also successfully synthesized focusing on the detection of Co2+ in spiked irrigation water near two factories. LOD is 0.26 μM and the linear range is 1–10 μM, far lower than the highest allowable concentration of Co2+ in irrigation water (8.5 mM) announced by Canadian Water Quality Guidelines. Despite the successful applications of MNCs-CDs and QDs-CDs toward Co2+ detection, OD-CDs composites have not been employed to the sensing of Co2+, which remains a room for further studies.

Cr6+ is one of the priority toxic pollutants in industries and harmful to plants, animals and humans, so quantitative detection of Cr6+ is quite important. FC-CDs composites have been prepared for the application of sensing Cr6+. In Ref. [64], a one-pot process for the preparation of fluorescent QDs-linked isophorone diisocyanate and β-CD composites by indirect covalent connection was designed toward the determination of Cr6+. Fluorescence quenching of the sensor occurs, which can be attributed to the chelation of Cr6+ with –COONH– and bridged bonds. This method can be applied in detection of Cr6+ in water (recovery of 95.8%–99.3 %) and soil (recovery of 93.59%–97.34 %), which successfully provides a valuable example for Cr6+ detection in water and soil samples.

Mn2+ is an essential trace element involved in calcium absorption and blood sugar regulation [127]. Nevertheless, an overconcentration of Mn2+ can inhibit iron absorption, and long-term excess can affect nervous system function. As a proof-of-concept, CTSCD and N-doped QDs composites were synthesized [76], providing a rapid and efficient sensor platform for Mn2+. The composites can quantitatively determine Mn2+ with the LOD value of 5.3 nM, which is lower than the national standard for drinking water (0.1 mg L−1). Interference studies indicate that the addition of Fe3+ and Hg2+ shows non-negligible influence for the detection of Mn2+, implying that the anti-interference capability of the nanocomposites is still on the way.

To deduce, FC-CDs composites have been successfully used for fluorescence sensing of Hg2+, Cu2+ Ag+, Au3+, Co2+ Cr6+, and Mn2+. Besides, their applications for other metal ions, e.g. Pd2+, Cd2+, VO2+ and Al3+, have also been reported [53,128,129]. It can be expected that more promising development of FC-CDs composites toward sensitive and selective metal ions sensing can be achieved in the future.

Unlike metal ions, only a few studies have been reported about the application of FC-CDs for sensing anions, typically I− and Cr2O7 2−. Iodine is an important element for the synthesis of thyroid hormone in human body [130]. Either excessive or insufficient iodine intake is associated with a spectrum of diseases like thyroid dysfunction and hypothyroidism [131]. Therefore, it is essential to develop an efficient and convenient method to detect I− concentration. In 2019, dihydrolipoic acid (DHLA)/β-CD and AgNCs composites were synthesized and employed for sensing I− in milk samples [132]. With the existence of I−, DHLA/β-CD-AgNCs composites are aggregated, accompanied by the decrease of the fluorescence intensity. The composites display wide linear range (0.2–60 μM) and low LOD (0.06 μM) for the detection of I−. This work facilitates the study of AgNCs as the fluorescent probe and shows practical application for I− detection in milk and drug samples. In addition, the sensitive detection of I− with (LOD of 1.44 μM) was also achieved by another FC-CD, mono-(6-diethylenetriamine-6-deoxy)-β-CD composites, which was prepared based on covalent connection [124].

Cr2O7 2− is a common form of the widespread toxic heavy metal Cr(VI) and has been classified as a group-I carcinogen by International Agency for Research on Cancer [133]. Thus, developing Cr2O7 2− sensors to realize the rapid and accurate monitor of Cr2O7 2− is of great desire. 1,5-Dihydroxyanthraquinone-β-CD composites as fluorescent chemosensors were developed and applied to Cr2O7 2− sensing with low LOD (0.8 nM) [71]. 1,5-Dihydroxyanthraquinone can enter into the hydrophobic cavity of CDs to form stable and water-soluble composites through covalent connection. The luminescence of 1,5-dihydroxyanthraquinone-β-CD is quenched by Cu2+ because of PET process, and only Cr2O7 2− can recover the fluorescence of the composites, based on which the sensitive detection of Cr2O7 2− is realized. Due to the advantages of high selectivity, sensitivity and good biocompatibility, the prepared sensor was successfully used for imaging in human lung cancer A549 cells.

As is well known, organic pollutants are toxic to human, animals, plants, and even influence the environment safety to some extent [134]. There is a growing interest in developing high-performance sensors to monitor the widespread use and harmful effects of organic pollutants in food and environment. So far, the fluorescence sensing of pesticides, antibiotics, nitroaromatic explosives and phenolic pollutants with FC-CDs composites has been reported.

Pesticides have been extensively used in the world because it can effectively protect the health of crops as much as possible [135,136]. However, they may do harm to food, environment, and can easily induce cancer and deformity to human beings, so achieving sensitive detection of pesticides in soil, water and food is essential. Up to now, most of reported fluorescent methods for pesticides sensing depend upon the enzyme catalytic or inhibition effect. Nevertheless, due to the disadvantages of enzyme including easy to inactivate, difficult to store, short-time to use and expensive, designing enzyme-free fluorescent platforms for pesticides sensing are of great value [137]. FC-CDs have found their applications in enzyme-free fluorescence sensing toward a number of commonly used pesticides, including malathion, paraoxon, parathion, paraquat and isocarbophos.

Malathion, a low-toxicity insecticide with good touching toxicity, is always used in the process of crops growing, which is inevitably harmful to human body [138]. A rapid, sensitive and accurate method based on AgNPs/β-CD and 2,3-dihydro-5-oxo-5H-thiazolo [3,2-α] pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) was developed for malathion recognition [103]. The fluorescence of TPCA was quenched by AgNPs/β-CD and the quenching reaction reached a balance within 5 min. Once malathion was added into the reaction system, the fluorescence of TPCA was recovered. The emission spectra of TPCA and the absorption spectra of AgNPs-β-CD largely overlap, proving that FRET is the main mechanism of fluorescence quenching. This method ensures good selectivity and sensitivity (LOD of 0.01 μg mL−1) for sensing malathion, providing a promising strategy for the enzyme-free sensing of pesticides.

Paraoxon remained in the environment can enter the human body and inhibit the acetylcholine esterase activity, which is extremely harmful [139]. It can hydrolyze to produce p-NP under alkaline conditions, which can easily enter into the hydrophobic cavity of CD to form a host-guest complex. Composites of 3-mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe QDs and β-CD coated with CTAB were prepared through electrostatic interaction [78], which effectively avoided time-consuming synthesis process. The paraoxon sensing system was realized by the decrease of fluorescence intensity and successfully applied in the rapid detection of paraoxon in body fluids. Besides, this approach possesses the merits for field implementation on-site without requiring expensive large equipment and trained employees, which is obviously superior compared to gas chromatography/mass spectrometry methods.

MP tends to accumulate in food and water because of its bioaccumulation and low solubility, which can lead to organ dysfunction, cancer disease and fetal abnormalities [140]. Similar to paraoxon, MP can be hydrolyzed to p-NP under alkaline conditions. As an example, composites of MoS2 QDs-β-CD were designed for MP sensing with LOD of 3.3 ppb [93]. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of p-NP on MoS2 QDs-β-CD can be attributed to PET process. This enzyme-free method can avoid the disadvantages of enzyme including relatively expensive and easily inactivated with short service life [141,142], and is more convenient to be used in practical sensing applications. In addition, MoS2 QDs-β-CD can distinguish MP from a series of other pesticides such as triazophos, dibrom, phosphamidon, glyphosate, proving itself to be a sensitive and promising sensor.

Paraquat is a highly toxic herbicide that can cause damage and death to vital organs such as respiratory system, liver, heart and kidneys [143]. A lot of efforts have been made to develop efficient methodologies for detecting paraquat in foods and environment. For instance, a simple, highly sensitive and selective fluorescent detection method for paraquat was developed using β-CD and GSH-capped AuNCs composites [95]. Paraquat is confined to the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD to form the host-guest complex and can quench the fluorescence of AuNCs-β-CD due to the PET process. AuNCs-β-CD composites have the advantages of easy preparation, wide linear range (5–100 ng mL−1 and 100–350 ng mL−1), low LOD (1.2 ng mL−1) and well-accepted recoveries (93.6%–103.8 %), proving that the probe has high application potential for paraquat sensing in water and vegetable samples.

Isocarbophos is a kind of broad-spectrum insecticidal and acaricidal agent, which has caused great pollution to the environment, food and water [144]. To develop highly sensitive methods for isocarbophos analysis in the field of food safety protection, fluorescence “turn-on” composites of β-CD polymer and pyrene were constructed for isocarbophos detection [22]. As isocarbophos concentration increases from 5 to 50 μg L−1, the emission intensity of the composites decreases and quenches gradually. The calculated LOD of the composites toward isocarbophos is 1.2 μg L−1. Besides, this method can be successfully used for the determination of isocarbophos residues in fruit samples due to their fantastic fluorescent properties and good sensitivity as well as selectivity, which is important for further practical applications.

As mentioned above, FC-CDs based strategies rely on interactions between the composites and pesticides or their hydrolysates, which can help much to meet the requirement of enzyme-free fluorescence sensing of pesticides. On the other hand, for the purpose of on-site testing pesticides in a portable way, fast and convenient methods for visually quantitative sensing pesticides based on facile and portable fluorescence capture devices including smartphone and test paper are still worth to explore.

Antibiotics have been widely and effectively used to prevent and treat certain diseases in organisms. Unfortunately, the overuse of antibiotics tends to cause the emergence of biological resistance and poses a threat to the health of consumers [145,146]. Therefore, rapid and effective detection of concentrations of antibiotics is essential for assessing risks to human health [147]. However, there are only several reports about the detection of antibiotics using FC-CDs, and only chlortetracycline (CTC) has been sensed.

CTC is one of the tetracycline antibiotics and often used to treat animal diseases [148]. However, the inappropriate use of antibiotics may cause damage to humans, like human toxicity, reproductive disorders and kidney disease [149]. Thus, the determination of CTC in food of animal origin should be taken into consideration. A red-emitting fluorescence sensor of AuNCs-β-CD was prepared and used for the sensitive detection of CTC in milk samples (Fig. 6 ) [104]. After CTC is introduced, the fluorescence of AuNCs-β-CD at 670 nm is declined. Results show that the fluorescence quenching mechanisms are IFE and FRET. The method has high selectivity, wide linear range (5 nM-1 μM) and low LOD value (2.7 nM), which can be applied to the paper-based sensing for rapid and convenient visual detection of CTC. Unfortunately, except CTC, there are few reports of FC-CDs for antibiotics detection by fluorescence sensing, and more attention should be paid in the future.

Selective and sensitive detection of nitroaromatic explosives is an important topic in the field of environmental monitoring because they pose a threat to human health and environmental safety. Nitroaromatic explosives such as p-NP, TNP, 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene (TNB) and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) are common ingredients in industrial explosives, which have been extensively sensed by FC-CDs [150].

p-NP is used in industry to produce a wide range of drugs and pesticides, so it is inevitably released and leads to the deterioration of the environment [151]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has added p-NP to its list of priority pollutants due to its persistence and toxicity [152], so it is of significance to monitor p-NP. FC-CDs have been proposed to determine the concentration of p-NP sensitively because the hydrophobic cavity of CDs can form a host-guest complex with p-NP, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence intensity. For example, the composites of SH-β-CD and AuNCs constructed by covalent connection could selectively and sensitively sense p-NP via the quenching of fluorescence signal by IFE mechanism [153]. After mixing the composites and p-NP for 30 s, the solution can be characterized directly by UV–Vis or fluorescence spectrophotometer, which fulfills the current ever-increasing demands of rapid detection. The developed approach exhibits high sensitivity (LOD of 90 nM), wide linear range (0.1–100.0 μM) and excellent selectivity, which demonstrates the potential for p-NP sensing in actual samples. Similar to the principle above, CdTe QDs-β-CD [154] and ZnO QDs-β-CD [155] were reported for the sensitive detection of p-NP, respectively. The obtained LOD values are both lower than that regulated by EPA's allowable limit for p-NP in the environment (0.43 μM). In addition, determining p-NP provides more possibilities for indirectly sensing other analytes, because p-NP is the hydrolyzed product of many substances, such as parathion [93], paraoxon mentioned above [78] and some proteins with hydrolyzed product of p-NP [1,92,107,156].

TNP, known as picric acid, is highly toxic and mutagenic even at low concentrations, and it is more explosive than TNT, a common explosive [80]. Thus, it is of significance to establish a rapid and reliable method for the detection of TNP. In a promising work, TPE-β-CD composites with a large stokes shift were designed by the functionalization of hydrophilic β-CD with a hydrophobic AIE group for sensitive TNP detection (LOD of 0.31 μM) [62]. The sensing mechanism is attributed to the combination of PET and FRET, with the PET mechanism dominating. TPE-β-CD test strips made by filter papers in the CH3OH solution (10−5 M) were also fabricated to achieve facile detection of TNP, demonstrating the practical application potential of the composites in real environment samples. This approach provides an economical, reliable, convenient and rapid method for TNP detection, and facilitates the further development of portable sensing devices.

In addition to the above single nitroaromatic explosives detection, it is also worth to identify a variety of nitro explosives at the same time. For example, TNT, TNB and TNP were discriminatively detected by self-assembled and pyrene-attached β-CD nanoaggregates in water via π-π stacking [157]. Besides, pirimicarb, a moderately toxic carbamate insecticide, was also detected selectively in the meantime.

Phenol and its derivatives have been widely used as intermediates in the biosynthesis of secondary metabolites of polyphenols and precursors in the industrial synthesis of organic compounds [158,159]. However, long-term exposure to phenolic pollutants in humans can lead to abnormal breathing, coma, and even death [160]. Therefore, effective and sensitive detection of phenolic pollutants in the environment has become a global problem that needs to be solved urgently [161].

Bisphenol A (BPA) is an intermediate chemical widely used in the synthesis of polycarbonate and epoxy resins, which is relevant to biological and toxicological hazards even at low doses Therefore, sensitive measurement of low levels of BPA is of great significance. As a typical example, pyrene/dimethyl-CD composites were developed and applied to selectively detecting BPA by competitive binding between pyrene and BPA [162]. The composites exhibit a dynamic detection range from 79 nM to 16.6 μM with the LOD of 70 nM, and possess excellent reproducibility, reversibility and selectivity. Additionally, this method provides a better understanding of host-guest chemistry and shows that supramolecular assembly can be used as a powerful tool for developing high-quality chemical sensors. In another study, an attempt was made to synthesize ZnO QDs-β-CD composites for BPA detection [163]. The probe can distinguish BPA from a series of other metal ions and organic molecules such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, methyl orange and 4-aminophenol, which proves itself to be a promising sensor.

Catechol (CC) and hydroquinone (HQ) are two positional isomers of phenolic compounds, which are important pollutants in medical, food and environmental matrices [164]. Therefore, reliable analytical procedures are required to determine CC and HQ with high sensitivity. QDs-β-CD composites were successfully developed for sensing CC and HQ in tap and lake water samples [51]. Due to the strong fluorescence and high degree of water solubility, QDs-β-CD composites are suitable as a nanoprobe for the detection of CC or HQ in tap and lake water samples. In addition, the cell viability of QDs-β-CD composites indicates that the composites affect the Hela cells only to a slight extent, which suggests that the composites can be potentially used for cell imaging applications.

In summary, due to the high sensitivity of FC-CDs, they have been introduced to different systems to sense organic pollutants. More and more FC-CDs are being developed and applied to the detection of organic pollutants in real samples, and we can expect that the detection of organic pollutants will be on-site, sensitive and convenient in the near future.

The abnormal concentration of some small molecules in body fluids is often closely related to the occurrence and development of diseases. These small molecules include cholesterol, glucose, DA, etc. [56,61,112,165,166]. In recent years, the sensing of small biomolecules based on FC-CDs has received more and more attention due to their high fluorescence intensity, excellent host-guest recognition ability and great water solubility. In addition, compared to some of the diagnostic methods possessing the disadvantages of being time-consuming, expensive and requiring skilled professionals [167], the sensing platform based on FC-CDs can help miniaturize biosensing platforms and need less sample volumes, shorter assay time and lower operating costs.

Steroids and their derivatives tested by FC-CDs include cholesterol, testosterone, and cholic acid (CA). The content elevating of cholesterol in the blood significantly increases the risk of cardiovascular disease [168]. Up to now, common methods for cholesterol sensing are always based on cholesterol oxidase [169,170]. However, cholesterol oxidase is prone to denaturation [171], so it is urgent to explore more efficient ways for sensing cholesterol. Many studies have shown that cholesterol can form host-guest complexes with FC-CDs and cause fluorescence changes, which is the basis for the detection by FC-CDs. For example, AuNCs-β-CD for detecting cholesterol with specific enzyme-free method was designed [56]. AuNCs-β-CD can form inclusion host-guest complexes with cholesterol, which enhances the rigidity of the molecules and fluorescence intensity. This method was successfully applied to the determination of cholesterol in actual serum samples with LOD of 5.77 μM and a relatively wide linear range of 10–100 μM. In another study, a fluorescence and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy dual-mode system was designed [112] and could satisfy the detection of cholesterol in the human body by using the competition between Rhodamine 6G and cholesterol binding with AuNPs-β-CD (Fig. 7 ). The fluorescence and surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) intensities of Rhodamine 6G linearly increase with cholesterol concentration, and the obtained LOD is 95 and 74 nM, respectively. Compared with reported single-mode methods like colorimetric [172] and electrochemical methods [173], this dual-mode strategy has obvious superiorities, including avoiding interference caused by instrument effectively, and achieving the flexibility of detection and multi-scene applications. In addition, QDs-β-CD [61], QDs-β-CD@AuNPs [166] and Rh6G@NU-1000-CMCD [89] were also used in sensing cholesterol with satisfactory results.

Testosterone is a kind of steroid hormone which is essential for male development and function, and its levels have been linked to a number of diseases, such as prostate cancer and kidney stones [174]. Hence, the accurate measurement of testosterone level in tissues and cells is important to understand the reproductive physiology. Fluorescence “turn-on” S,N codoped QDs-β-CD composites were developed for testosterone sensing [60]. The fluorescence intensity of the composites linearly responds to the concentration range of testosterone from 0 to 280 μM and the LOD is 0.51 μM. Although currently established methods can meet the demands of sensitivity and selectivity in the assays of testosterone [175,176], considering no need of tedious sample pretreatments and sophisticated equipment, this method is suitable for the routine rapid determination of testosterone in environmental water samples. Besides, due to their low cytotoxicity, high anti-interference ability and excellent biocompatibility, the composites were successfully used for 293T cells imaging and monitoring the levels of testosterone in the cytoplasm of cells, which has a promising application value in medical research.

CA is a kind of sterol derivatives, which is the essential substance of human fat metabolism [177]. The concentration of CA is usually below 10 μM in human serum, and abnormal CA concentration is associated with liver cancer, gallstone and other diseases, so developing methods for sensing CA is urgently needed. For example, with specific host-guest recognition ability, N-doped QDs-β-CD composites were designed for CA sensing [108] CA can compete the cavity of β-CD with Fc+ and is able to stop PET process and recover the fluorescence of QDs-β-CD. The composites have the advantages of easy preparation, wide linear range (0–650 μM) and LOD (25 nM), and are suitable for the CA detection in human serum and urine with an average recovery rate of 97.1%–103.4 %. Besides, although the obtained LOD is higher than that of ultra-HPLC-MS/MS [178] and pressurized capillary electrochromatography-laser-induced fluorescence [179], this method has the merits of fast response without any complex pretreatment steps, a broader linear range and can be used to accurately detect CA above the normal level in the human body (<10 μM) and thus identify hepatobiliary diseases, which endows the composites with the potential in clinical and medical applicability.

Glucose levels can determine whether organisms suffer from diabetes mellitus, a famous chronic disease [180]. Therefore, the development of a fast, simple, accurate and sensitive method to monitor glucose levels in the blood or urine is vital and extremely necessary for human health. In 2012, the composites consisting of long and hydrophobic stilbeneboronic acid and γ-CD were developed for the sensitive detection of glucose by fluorescence enhancement due to the increased rigidity [70]. The sensor was utilized to determine glucose in artificial urine sample to evaluate its practicability, indicating that it is broadly applicable to the quantitative detection of glucose in complicated samples. In addition, a ZnS QDs-β-CD probe was fabricated and used for glucose sensing in human serum and urine samples via FRET mechanism [111]. There is a good linear relationship in the glucose concentration ranged from 0.4 mM to 1.4 mM, and high selectivity and low LOD (84 nM) were achieved. Besides, this approach satisfies the demands of practical sample analysis including human serum and urine samples, showing potential applications for detecting glucose in other matrices.

Dopamine, as one of the most crucial monoamine neurotransmitters, plays an important role in human health [88,181]. Dopamine can be detected successfully by MNCs/QDs/MOFs-CDs mainly due to its reducibility and host-guest recognition with CDs. A simple, economically viable and “green” method was developed for the synthesis of AuNCs-β-CD composites in 2018 [50]. This method has wide linear range (100 nm-80 μM), low LOD (20 nM) and excellent recoveries (95.02%–103.25 %), making it successful to measure dopamine in human serum samples and showing great practicability for dopamine detection in biological and pharmaceutical fields. QDs-CDs such as N-doped G QDs-β-CD [85,182] and ZnO QDs-β-CD [183] were also employed for dopamine detection, which can achieve the LOD required for disease control and is promising to sense dopamine in clinical laboratories and biomedical research. Besides, Eu-α-CD MOF on nitrocellulose membrane test strips [88] can realize the visual semi-quantitative detection of DA in a fast and high-selectivity way, demonstrating its great potential in decentralized analysis or in point-of-care applications.

Histamine, an important biogenic amine, can cause severe toxicological effects to human beings in excessive amounts, so it is necessary to test it in different foods and beverages [184]. ZnO QDs-β-CD composites modified by vitamin B6 cofactors including pyridoxal 5′-phosphate (PLP) and pyridoxal (Py) were prepared and successfully used in histamine sensing [185]. With the addition of histamine, the aldehyde group of the ZnO QDs surface modified by PLP or Py can react with the amine group of histamine, which significantly enhances fluorescence in the blue-green region. The two sensors have wide linear range (2.49–24.4 μM and 7.44–47.6 μM), high sensitivity (0.59 μM and 0.97 μM), and high selectivity to free histamine without suffering from the interference of other biogenic amines. However, no practical samples were tested to prove the practicability of the two sensors, and whether they are promising for further application in sensing histamine or not remains to be identified.

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) plays a crucial role for many biological phenomena and biochemical pathways and ensures the supply of energy. Abnormal levels of ATP in cells can lead to certain diseases, including malignant tumors, vascular cardiomyopathy, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease [186]. The sensitive detection of ATP by designing β-CD functionalized CuInS2 QDs composites and ATP aptamer was reported [187]. Once ATP is introduced into the system of CuInS2 QDs-β-CD and ATP aptamer, aptamer-ATP complexes can enter into the hydrophobic cavities of β-CD and result in the great enhancement of fluorescence intensity. The composites show a good linear response (6–1200 μM) and LOD (3 μM) toward ATP detection. Although the LOD of the approach is not lower than that found in the previous reports [188,189], the selectivity and simplicity of the method are quite remarkable. To overcome the drawback of being easily affected by environmental factors due to single fluorescence change, polyanionic sulphated β-CD anchored by thioflavin-T [190], a ratiometric fluorescence probe, was designed for the detection of ATP. Compared to some conventional fluorescent sensors which follow sophisticated protocols and time-consuming synthesis procedures [191,192], thioflavin-T is more preferred in practical application due to its advantages of low price and commercial availability. The fluorescence increases reasonably in a linear dynamic range from 0 to 150 μM and the calculated LOD is 1.3 μM. In addition, CuInS2 QDs-β-CD and thioflavin-T-β-CD composites were successfully applied to ATP detection in biological systems such as human serum samples with good selectivity and sensitivity, demonstrating their broad application prospects.

In summary, with fascinating fluorescence behaviors, FC-CDs find their sensing applications toward many small molecules and show expansive prospects. Nevertheless, it should be noted that FC-CDs have not been extensively used in clinic laboratories. One of the possible reasons is that the sensitivity of fluorescence method is not as excellent as the powerful chromatographic-mass spectrometric method in clinical laboratories [193], so it is vital to develop sensitive approaches based on FC-CDs for monitoring small biological molecules. Besides, the toxicity and stability researches of some FC-CDs remain unstudied. In the process of fabricating FC-CDs, hypotoxic fluorescence probes (e.g. MNCs) are more preferred. Accordingly, the sensing application of FC-CDs in clinical laboratories is still in the academic development stage, and more attention needs to be paid.

The abnormal concentration of biomacromolecules such as proteins in the body fluids of organisms can lead to the occurrence of diseases. Currently, proteins in organism sensing by FC-CDs include acid/alkaline phosphatase (ACP/ALP), β-galactosidase (β-Gal), α-amylase and BSA.

ACP, considered as an important serum marker, has been adopted as an auxiliary tool in the preliminary pathological diagnosis of many diseases [194]. In order to offer a rapid, continuous and real-time detection, a FRET biosensor by attaching nile red to G QDs linked by lecithin/β-CD for the detection of ACP activity was established [195]. As a connector, lecithin/β-CD shortens the distance between nile red and G QDs, which is helpful to experience the FRET process, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of G QDs and the fluorescence enhancement of nile red. Once ACP is added, lecithin will be separated into two parts and the FRET process will be prevented. After optimizing parameters that affect the performance of the sensor (e.g., excitation and emission wavelengths, incubation time for the formation of lecithin/β-CD and nile red. etc.), the sensor exhibits a linear response in the range of 0–1500 μU mL−1 and the LOD is 28 μU mL−1 for ACP detection. Furthermore, high selectivity was achieved in the presence of diverse coexistence substances, which confirms the potential application of sensing ACP in complex samples.

ALP activity is often regarded as an important diagnostic biomarker and a common enzyme in clinical diagnosis [196]. ALP can catalyze p-nitrophenylphosphate (NPP) to produce p-NP, which can easily enter into the cavity of β-CD. As an attempt shown in Fig. 8 , AgNCs-β-CD [92] was designed to achieve high sensitivity of ALP detection according to the fluorescence quenching by IFE (Fig. 8). This method displays a linear range (0.02–10.0 U L−1) and a quite low LOD (0.0046 U L−1). In short, AgNCs-β-CD has great potential in sensing and cell imaging applications due to its merits of facile preparation and good biocompatibility. Another composite, hydropopyl β-CD linked to boronic acid functionalized QDs by B–O chemical bonds, was developed for ALP sensing [107]. The correlation between the decreased fluorescence intensity and ALP concentration can be used to establish quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a wide linear range from 3.4 to 100.0 U L−1 with the low LOD of 0.9 U L−1. These strategies broaden the application of host-guest recognition for ALP activity detection in clinical practice. Accordingly, host-guest recognition between β-CD and p-NP can be expected to develop more diverse and sensitive biosensors in practical detection.

β-Gal is one of the important biomarkers of ovarian cancer detection, so reports of its detection have induced a wide spread of discussion [197]. Similar to the above recognition approach of sensing ALP, as a hydrolysate of 4-nitrophenyl-β-d-galactopyranoside, p-NP can form inclusion complex with β-CD, inducing the fluorescence changes of sensors. In previous studies, QDs-β-CD [156] and CuNCs-β-CD [1] were used to determine the activity assay of β-Gal in ovarian cancer cells and human serum sample, respectively. Sensitive assays for β-Gal determination in terms of linear correlation of the fluorescence change with the β-Gal level were established with high sensitivity (LOD of 0.6 and 0.56 U L−1 corresponding to QDs-β-CD and CuNCs-β-CD) and practicality, which can also be expanded to the detection of other enzymes. This sensing platform shows superiority in sensitivity, which benefits from the highly efficient energy transfer in the sensor system [198,199].

α-amylase is one of the important enzymes involved in starch hydrolysis [200]. FC-CDs composites with TPE motif and γ-CD based supramolecular fluorometric sensing system was established for sensing α-amylase [116]. The benzene ring of the system was restricted to free rotation in γ-CD, so no signal was emitted in the water-based medium. However, when α-amylase was added into the system, γ-CD was hydrolyzed and the TPE motif was released, resulting in the recovered fluorescence. To evaluate the capability of the composites in practical application, the probe with low LOD (0.007 U mL−1) and wide linear range (0–0.35 U mL−1) was successfully applied to detect α-amylase in real human serum samples. The range of α-amylase level in healthy people is 0.019–0.051 U mL−1 and that in acute pancreatitis patients is 0.163–0.287 U mL−1, thus, the proposed composites have practical applications for the determination of α-amylase in clinical diagnostics.

To the best of our knowledge, there are not many studies focusing on FC-CDs for the assay of BSA in recent years. So far, only Zhu's group performed fluorescence sensing of BSA by using ODs-CDs in 2006 and 2007. They firstly reported tetrachloride fluorescein (2,4,5,7-tetrachloro-3,6-flfluorandiol) composites based on β-CD for BSA sensing in 2006 [201]. Under the optimum conditions, the linear range and LOD are 0.0–28.0 μg mL−1 and 0.76 μg mL−1, respectively. Subsequently, they designed the composites of erythrosine sodium and hydroxypropyl-β-CD by host-guest connection [202], which showed great fluorescence response to BSA. The fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase of BSA concentration in the range of 0.5–32.0 μg mL−1, and the LOD is as low as 49.2 ng mL−1. Both of the methods were used for the determination of BSA in human serum samples with satisfactory results. Besides, compared with Bradford assay, one of the most commonly used protein colorimetric assays [203], the above methods possess the advantages of high sensitivity and convenience, which have great potential for precise and accurate detection of BSA, as well as exceptional miniaturization possibilities for equipment-free and on-site sensing.

Researchers have also developed fluorescence probes for detecting uric acid [204], tartrazine [114], acridine orange [40], malachite green [205] and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural [206] based on FC-CDs. These FC-CDs have good sensitivity, selectivity, stability, biocompatibility, and can be applied to actual samples, showing great potential applications in the field of biochemistry. Besides, although FC-CDs have been widely considered as potential biomolecular sensors applied in clinical detection, few of them (e.g., TPE-CD [116]) can successfully distinguish patients from normal people in practical analysis. Therefore, developing more highly sensitive FC-CDs for clinical detection and diagnosis is still on the way.

Temperature is an important physical parameter, and its accurate measurement is of great significance for human life and natural ecology [207]. Up to now, most FC-CDs for temperature sensing are MNCs-CDs. The composites of β-CD and GSH-AuNCs linked by covalent connection were prepared [94]. They are not limited to accurately sense changes in temperature, but can also simultaneously identify other ions like Pb2+, Fe3+, Ag+ which cannot be achieved by conventional temperature sensors (e.g., thermocouple, semiconductor thermometer and thermistor) [208]. The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs-β-CD decreases gradually with increasing temperature in the range from 20 to 50 °C. Moreover, with the merits of high chemical stability and low toxicity, AuNCs-β-CD probe can selectively label MGC-803 cells, which has promising potential to be applied in the diagnosis and in-situ treatment of gastric cancer cells.

Intracellular pH plays a number of key roles in cell, enzyme and tissue activity, so pH changes need to be fast track in cells to prevent certain failure occurring [209]. Similar to temperature sensing, MNCs-CDs have also found their wide applications to pH sensing. pH sensitive and non-cytotoxic AuNCs composites coated with DLHA incorporated γ-CD were developed [210]. The composites underwent a significant wavelength shift at slightly acidic pH. Changes of DLHA configuration on the AuNCs surface may lead to the expulsion of γ-CD as pH variation, resulting in the shift of emission wavelength. And this strategy is also used in bioimaging in human breast cancer (MCF-7) and Huh cells, which seems to be helpful for the selective detection of tumor cells with high precision.

As mentioned above, FC-CDs have many applications for fluorescent sensing including inorganic ions, organic pollutants, bio-related molecules, temperature and pH. What we can get is that by compositing CDs and different kinds of fluorescent materials, synergistic effect can be achieved, and great potential in FC-CDs sensing can be foreseen. It can be expected that FC-CDs sensors can be applied to daily life as soon as possible to bring more convenience to human beings.

Imaging refers to the reflect of different kinds of imaging techniques combined with detection targets to image cells, tissues and organisms to obtain biological information, which plays a significant role in diagnosis and therapies [211]. FC-CDs exhibit desirable biocompatibility, which is embodied in low cytotoxicity and efficient cell transmittance, so they are likely to imaging in different kinds of cells.

Hela cells are one of the most commonly used imaging cells due to their characteristics of unlimited and rapid replication [212]. In Yan's study [213], CoOOH nanosheet and a trans-4-[p-(N,N-diethylamino)styryl]-N-methylpyridinium (DEASPI) dye with β-CD nanoprobe was reported, which was capable of sensitive fluorescence detection and three-dimensional (3D) imaging of ascorbic acid (AA) in biological samples. CoOOH nanosheet was placed on the surface of DEASPI, as both a fluorescence quenching agent and AA recognition unit. In the presence of AA, CoOOH nanosheet was reduced by Co (II), which triggered the fluorescence recovery. The in vitro imaging procedure of endogenous AA was demonstrated in living Hela cells. In addition, 3D imaging of exogenous AA in tissues up to a depth of 320 μm was also performed by two-photon excitation microscopy (Fig. 9 ). Although the cost of two-photon imaging and the limitation of the instrument bring certain challenges to this approach in the practical analysis, this nanoprobe represents a valuable tool for elucidating the role of AA in clinical studies.

HepG2 cells were derived from liver cancer in a 15-year-old white man, and the biotransforming metabolic enzymes contained in HepG2 cells are homologous to human normal liver parenchymal cells [214]. Therefore, HepG2 cells are often used as ideal cell lines for hepatocyte metabolism or genotoxicity tests in vitro. For instance, QDs-β-CD composites were prepared by a one-pot method and successfully used for the determination of isoniazid and applied in HepG2 cellular bioimaging [49]. In another report, inclusion composites 3,3′-dihydroxybenzidine (DHB) and α-CD [105] were prepared for sensitive detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by chelating with nitrogen of –NH2 and oxygen of –OH. DHB was stable and provided intramolecular charge transfer within the composites from the electron donor to the electron acceptor group to obtain chemical reactivity. It has a great potential application in Hg2+ and Fe3+ HepG2 cells imaging.

Besides, imaging in other cells such as L929 cells [126], 293T cells [60], PC-3M cells [195], MCF-7 cells [92,210], A549 cells [71] and OVCAR3 cells [156] has also been reported by FC-CDs as described above. These FC-CDs, possessing good stability and biocompatibility, can successfully enrich their applications in the field of in vitro bioimaging.

FC-CDs with low biotoxicity and high stability tend to cluster on target organs for in vivo bioimaging. Up to now, there are only a few researches about in vivo bioimaging of FC-CDs. A possible reason may be that the stability of MNCs-CDs remains to be studied [215], and QDs-CDs have a shorter shelf life due to their micellar instability [216]. ODs-CDs are more stable and less toxic, so they have found applications for in vivo imaging.

Renal-clearable macromolecular composites consisting of infrared fluorescence hemi-cyanine substrate coupled alkyne-functionalized hydroxypropyl β-CD were reported [217].
